1:
Ps 53: The Elohist's Psalm 14. Ps 53 is nearly identical to Ps 14 but belongs to the 'Elohist Psalter' (Pss 42-83), which systematically uses Elohim where the rest of the Psalter uses YHWH. The reason for the Elohist Psalter is debated: possibly a liturgical variant for contexts where the divine name was not spoken, or from a different editorial tradition. The superscription adds 'On Mahalath' — a musical term of uncertain meaning (possibly the name of a tune, a type of instrument, or 'for sickness'). 'A Maskil of David' — the instructional psalm label.

'The fool has said in his heart, There is no God' — nabal (fool) is the moral fool of wisdom literature: not the intellectually challenged but the one who is morally obtuse, practically ungodly. Nabal famously appears in 1 Sam 25 (the man whose name was Nabal, meaning 'fool'); the word designates the person who acts without reference to God's moral order. 'In his heart' — not a public atheistic declaration but a private, practical god-lessness: acting as if YHWH does not observe or judge.
2: 'God looks down from heaven on the children of man, to see if there are any who understand, who seek after God' — the divine survey: YHWH looks for genuine seekers (dorshei Elohim, those who pursue God) and finds none. The divine searching is a judgment: the creator who made humans to seek him finds universal practical atheism. This is the same divine survey as in Genesis 18:21 (YHWH investigating Sodom) — the judge descending to examine the evidence.
3: 'They have all fallen away; together they have become corrupt; there is none who does good, not even one' — hakol sar yachdav ne'elahu (all have turned aside; together they have become corrupt/sour) — the universal apostasy. Paul cites v3 (and Psalms 14:3) in Romans 3:12 as part of his catena of OT texts establishing universal human sinfulness (Romans 3:10-18). The 'not even one' (ein gam echad) emphasizes totality: no exception.
4: 'Have those who work evil no knowledge, who eat up my people as they eat bread and do not call upon God?' — 'eating my people like bread' — the exploitation metaphor of predatory consumption: the powerful feed on the poor as casually as eating daily bread. Micah 3:3 uses similar language ('who eat the flesh of my people'). ANE economic systems allowed the wealthy to consume the resources of the poor through debt, foreclosure, and labor exploitation. The exploiters 'do not call upon God' — batach vocabulary is absent: they have no covenant relationship that could moderate their predation.
5: 'There they are, in great terror, where there is no terror! For God scatters the bones of him who encamps against you; you put them to shame, for God has rejected them' — the divine reversal: the oppressors experience 'great terror' in a place 'where there is no terror' — the divine panic (cf. Psalms 48:5) strikes where no objective threat exists. 'God scatters the bones' — the most radical military victory image: not just defeat but the total disintegration of the enemy's physical remains, which were left unburied (the greatest ANE disgrace). The variant from Ps 14 here is notable: Ps 14 reads differently in v5, suggesting this variant has a different historical or liturgical application.
6: 'Oh, that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion! When God restores the fortunes of his people, let Jacob rejoice, let Israel be glad' — the eschatological longing: the universal diagnosis of human sinfulness (vv1-4) ends not in despair but in prayer for divine salvation. 'Restores the fortunes' (shivit shevut) — the restoration-from-exile formula: the same phrase used in Jeremiah 29:14; Ezekiel 39:25; Amos 9:14 for the eschatological return from captivity. The psalm's cry for Israel's salvation from within the context of universal human failure is the precise prayer that the NT announces has been answered in Christ.
